speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you're going to tap me at two minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to tap you-- I'm going to tap you at three minutes. That's what I've been doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two?

speaker: interviewer
text: For me, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Because I-- I have enough trouble judging two minutes. And I think I've almost got that pat. [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm ready. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going to get close? No, not yet. First, you do the slate, just the slate. Just focus in. And I'll read it, and then I'll go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. Go. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Aren't you going to say the slate?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 27, 1997. The survivor is Sonia Sklaver. Name at birth, Kritczmar. The interviewer is Ruth Meyer. We are in Fair Lawn, New Jersey in the United States. And the interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going to get on camera with her?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, turn it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doesn't matter. You can get on camera with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stay where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now I need to get motion when you're taping.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're recording right now. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Hi, I'm Ruth Meyer, M-E-Y-E-R. We are with Sonia Sklaver. Today is March 27, 1997. We are in Fair Lawn, New Jersey and in the United States. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE] I'm going to set up the shot, because I have one camera with me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you know how we begin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. And I'm just going to focus on her. It's good. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sklaver, I'd like to begin by having you say your name for us and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is-- now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name now, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Sklaver, S-O-N-I-A. Sklaver, S-K-L-A-V-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: My name at birth was Shaindel Kritczmar, S-H-A-I-N-D-E-L. Kritczmar, K-R-I-T-C-Z-M-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 5, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now at this taping?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was the city and country you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the town of Hoszcza. At that time, 1925, it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you spell Hoszcza. For us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hoszcza has many spelling. It has-- in the Polish spelling, it's H-O-S-Z-C-Z-A, Hoszcza. They used to say that it had lots of mud. In Hoszcza, there were very thick mud. The word Hoszcza means [NON-ENGLISH], which is apparently what they had in mind. We-- we call it Hoscht, H-O-S-C-H-T, Hoscht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Poland when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it change hands at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times. Poland was many times in-- occupied by the Soviet-- not the Soviet-- the czarist Russia and Austria. Austria. And Poland was established as an independent state in 1920. Between 1918 and 1920, it was a Polish state. It lasted until 1939 until the Second World War broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to tell me your father's name please and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mordechai in Hebrew. Mordko, they called him in Polish. And Mordko, M-O-R-D-K-O. Mordechai, M-O-R-D-C-H-I-- A-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kritczmar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hinda, H-I-N-D-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Goldbitter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gold, G-O-L-D. And bitter, B-I-T-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a brother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were at four children-- a brother, Abraham. [NON-ENGLISH], he was called for short and born 1911. My sister was 1913. Her name was Perel. [NON-ENGLISH], called by the Polish people. And [NON-ENGLISH] remained mainly in-- in her life now even. And Leah was [NON-ENGLISH] sometimes called or Leah. And I was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother and sisters survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother left in 1932 as a [NON-ENGLISH] with a young bride to Israel. And he, of course, survived. At time, it was Palestine called. It became an independent state in 1948. And he has two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister left for Russia just as the Soviets were-- were, you know, fleeing when Germany attacked them. That was 1941-- in the spring of 1941. And she survived. Her husband was in the army all through the war. And he-- they didn't find each other until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was under Stalingrad. The Russian Army. Stalingrad, he was. He was wounded in the eye-- seriously wounded. And my sister Leah, she was with, you know, almost until the end. She survived. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] actually, she didn't survive. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was close when the-- when the Germans were fleeing. Apparently, there was a fire in the village. And when she was discovered, they reported her to the authorities. I had the regards from people that worked. They were shoe makers. And they-- very highly regarded in the villages, because they were sewing shoes and boots for the farmers. They were from the next town to ours, Tuchyn. And they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw her. They did not know that she was hiding. They maybe suspected, but they didn't know she was hiding until that fire that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fire was close to the liberation [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the winter of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me a little bit about what it was like at home. Can you describe your house for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- at first, we had a very small, little house, not too big, but enough for us. And the ceiling was always-- always like a-- a stomach kind I had. [LAUGHS] And I was afraid it going to fall some day. But finally, we had a very beautiful home built between 1930 and 1932. A new, beautiful home. We had a tenant. And they were always the military people, the tenants. In other words, we had a military garrison stationed in Hoszcza. We were on the border. And that's why the military was there. And that was the Polish military I'm talking about. They [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they had-- the, you know, the-- the couple would leave there. But the husband would go to the military. There was a large park with concerns or something like that with building. The park was off-limits to the Jewish people. Only on the days of the 1st of May-- not the 1st, the 3rd of May. That's when the-- the Polish people celebrated independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, everyone was allowed to go into the park. Was it always off-limits to the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always off-limits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always off-limits. And I wouldn't say maybe not just to the Polish people-- to the Jewish people. To the Polish people just as-- to the Ukrainian, I would say. All civilians were off-limits and Jews, of course. There was a-- a large gate. And it was a-- gated all over. One gate was, I remember, on farther part of the town, which was more on the outskirts. And one was in the town that I will show you the picture later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the part of the house that you lived in. What-- tell me what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very beautiful place with a backyard and lots of greens and trees and a small little brook running. And my oldest brother-- my older brother, he was always out of the house. He was in school. He was in the town of Rowne. He was then at gymnasium. And my sister, too, was in another town. And she was in Ostrog and studying, also gymnasium. My sister Leah and I were always at home with-- there is this-- my mother would prepare the goods, the challah, and the good kefirs to bake. Early, early in the morning Friday, she would have the oven lit and bake the good stuff. And we would tasted a very fresh [NON-ENGLISH], which was something like a [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it a cake or a cookie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, flatbread something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see. You said she lit the oven. Was it a wood oven, a coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wood. Wood, right. Big oven. Except that, of course, the heating was also done in special ovens. Everything with wood. Most of the time, wood, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- we had a store, a yard good business, retail. My mother was with him in the store. And I would help out also sometimes. Run in after school to get some money for ice cream or soda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and father worked together in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the Ukrainians and very few Polish people and of course, the Jewish population. Our town was-- was in the middle and surrounded by-- by Ukrainians, villagers. The town had a mall, I would say-- something like a modern mall-- with stores on each side of the-- of the long, long building, all under one roof. Across from the stores was a pharmacy, my uncle's pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your father's yard goods-- yard goods store--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right across from the pharmacy--

speaker: interviewer
text: In this little mall?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the mall. Next to him was his-- my uncle's-- his brother's store. And a little farther was my husband's, Irving's, store-- hardware store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big-- what was the population of Hoszcza?

speaker: interviewee
text: Population was 1,000 Jewish people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there also non-Jews living in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In town, only in the park. Those were the military and their families. Mainly their families lived on the outside of the park. And that's how we had a tenant, which was [INAUDIBLE] but had a beautiful, large dog, Dalmatian. And he was my-- I was his favorite, and he was mine. None of the Jewish children had dogs. But we had lots of cats. Cats for the mice to protect. And I had many, many cats. The mother, the father, the children. They called me [NON-ENGLISH], which means the Mother of the Cat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to tell me about your mother's preparations for Shabbos-- that she would bake early on Friday morning. What else do you remember about Shabbos in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Friday evening, my father-- we would have dinner early, Shabbos meal. And he would put his warm, warm [INAUDIBLE] on during the winter. [NON-ENGLISH] is a coat that had the fur inside. And on the outside was just a wool cloth. And he would walk to shul. The shul was-- everything was within walking distance in our town. And he would study missionaries, [INAUDIBLE] with them. Come very late home. And in the morning, he would go back to shul in the morning-- Shabbos morning. I do not remember that my mother went to shul on Shabbos. But on the holidays, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: The holidays were wonderful, really wonderful. Starting from the spring, Purim. Purim was-- was especially interesting, because the out of town young people would come and perform for-- for our town. That was probably for food stock and for-- for charity. But at first, I would be afraid when I was very young, you know. They would come in in outfits and costumes. And later, I loved it, because they sang and they performed and they would leave, you know. They would be fed first, and then they would leave. Then Passover was such an ordeal of preparing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the ordeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the Passover stuff was stored, part of fit, in the buffet in the living room, dining room area. And the rest, pots and pans, in the attic. And everything would come down and be washed and [NON-ENGLISH]. Matzo was-- was prepared in-- in special places of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] matzo baking. It was baked by hand at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it bake-- did they bake it in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Town. Town, yeah. It was large ovens. And I would run in to see and taste a piece of matzo. Sometimes a lot of children from the school would also go and participate and help in matzo baking. And we had a very nice Seders. Two hour Seder would come my aunt from Dombrovitz. She and her husband and the two children and [? Gania. ?] And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Uncle [INAUDIBLE], [CRYING] his two children, [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is a survivor. She survived in Russia, and she's now in Washington DC. Yosef, her brother, is somewhere in Russia. We-- we don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if he survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're not sure. Not sure. We have never heard from him, although he knows where we are. And he knows that he has a family in Washington DC. And this Seder was conducted until late. I would always fall asleep. Always fall asleep in the chair. For the-- for the holidays would come my brother [NON-ENGLISH], as long as he was still in Poland, and my sister Perel from gymnasium. They both arrived. So the family celebrated. And the very first thing after Passover, I couldn't fast enough run to the baker to get a piece of bread. Passover was not a good time [LAUGHTER] for my diet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you did have guests for the Seder. And did they stay with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed, of course. The town was, I would say, quite a distance from us. And that was the name Dombrovitz. It was not Ukraine. It was Policja, which is north from Ukraine. They would come by that time, I think, a train up to Rowne, and take buses to the town of Hoszcza. And Shabbos was exceptionally beautiful, because we were near a very large river, [NON-ENGLISH]. Around the river, there were pastures. And at times, it was covered all with-- with white flowers, daisies. And then it was the buttercups in yellow. And we would get lots of flowers to the house. Some homes, I remember, in Hoszcza did not have any wooden floors yet. They had dirt that every-- before Shabbos they would spray a certain mixture and freshly, like, paint it. But we had wooden floors already. As a matter of fact, we even had running water. In the very first-- in the old house, we even had a radio, which was with-- with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Headphones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Headphones. At times, I would fall asleep and schlep the whole thing with me. My brother was, you know, instrumental in getting that. And at that time, I remember also the electricity in town. No telephones, just those new inventions.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had electricity in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electricity, yes, even in the old house and in the new house, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only running water. Running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there wasn't a toilet or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- and where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was down in the basement. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But there was a laundry room with a-- a zinc metal bathtub. Laundry was done not too often. There was always a lady that would come and help with the laundry. And the [INAUDIBLE]-- and in the-- I would say like every season, a seamstress would come to sew linen. I don't think you could have bought them in any other store. So she would, you know, sew them to-- to each, you know, whatever-- whatever could be fixed, she would repair. And what not, make new pillowcases, new-- new sheets, new quilt covers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she also sew clothing for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not this one. Another one. Another one. She was an old lady, lived out of town. But she loved to sew cute little dresses for the-- for the townspeople. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about school. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: School-- I started with a public school. Very few Jewish people-- Jewish children were in the public school. They had-- they had mainly Ukrainian and a few Polish children. The school-- everything was within walking distance. And I did not stay there more than until three to four grade, because all my friends started to attend the [NON-ENGLISH], the Hebrew school in-- that was established in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one already 1918, which my sister and brother probably attended. But the last one was established in the-- in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Tarbut, which means education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went four years to the first school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The public school. Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Polish and Ukrainian was taught.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had private-- private lessons at home of Hebrew. But then I did not have to have the private lessons at home, because Tarbut school had-- had everything in Hebrew, except the Polish language and Polish history, because this is to be accredited as a-- by the government, the Polish government. That's why we had to have Polish language as a language and-- and Polish history also in Polish. All other subjects were in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the public school that you went to before Tarbut, who were you friendly with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was friendly there with the Christian children, Ukrainian and Polish. I visited their homes, and they visited our home. So though they were-- lived on the outskirts of the town-- the town. And I was a little bit uncomfortable there, because especially my name being Shaindel, they made fun of me. And I-- I did have Jewish friends. The Jewish girl friends lived very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in that school-- the first school-- did you have any anti-Semitic experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say an experience that I could remember. But I know that they made fun of the Jewish children. Yes. I was never harmed. And I was in very-- I was in very good relations with the non-Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was anti-Semitism in town, and in our town. Definitely yes, there was. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- and it showed up later on during the Holocaust. No one but no one wanted to-- to take-- to hide or to save Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were younger, before the war began, what was the relationship between the Jews and the Gentiles in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The relationship between the Jews and the Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Before-- before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had very-- they-- they would come to town to shop. In our store-- in my parents' store, they had credit. And when the harvest would be collected and sold, then they had the money to pay. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father did business with both Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. Mostly Ukrainians. Mostly Ukrainians, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop here and continue in a moment.
